title: What is Web 2.0?
body:
### Web 2.0 represents the evolution from traditional applications to end-user-focused web applications. Web 2.0 is a mindset rather than a specific technology.

When the web first began, we were in a static environment, with HTML pages that were rarely updated and lacked user interaction. **Web 2.0** **Web 2.0 is the transition from traditional applications to web-based applications focused on the end user. These are applications that foster collaboration and services that replace desktop applications.** It all began when Dale Dougherty of O’Reilly Media used this term at a conference where he brainstormed with Craig Cline of MediaLive, discussing the rebirth and evolution of the web. New applications and sites with surprising features were constantly emerging. And so the stage was set for the 2004 Web 2.0 Conference. This conference was not only successful but was followed up by the 2005 Web 2.0 Conference held in October. The opening talk at the Web Conference covered:

-   Blogs (Personal Pages)
-   Domain speculation --> Search engine optimization
-   Page views --> Cost per click
-   CMSs --> Wikis (Content management)
-   Categories/Directories --> Tagging

**What technologies support Web 2.0?** Web 2.0 doesn’t exactly mean there’s a formula for all our web applications to fit into this framework. However, there are several technologies currently in use that we should examine more closely in order to continue evolving alongside the web. ![web 2.0](http://www.xarop.com/content/img/meme3.jpg "web 2.0") **Some technologies that bring a Web 2.0 project to life:**

> 1. Migrating desktop software to the web platform. 2. Adherence to XHTML standards. 3. Separation of content from design using style sheets. 4. Content syndication. 5. Ajax (Asynchronous JavaScript and XML). 6. Use of Flash, Flex, or Lazo. 7. Use of Ruby on Rails to program dynamic pages. 8. Use of social networks to manage users and communities. 9. Giving users full control over their information. 10. Providing APIs or XML so that applications can be manipulated by others. 11. Facilitating search engine optimization with simple URLs.

**How Does Web 2.0 Benefit Us?** The term “Web 2.0” is currently in vogue, placing significant emphasis on a trend that has been present for some time. On the Internet, speculation has led to major tech bubbles and caused many projects to fail. Furthermore, our projects must be renewed and evolve. Web 2.0 isn’t exactly a technology; rather, it’s the mindset we must adopt when developing for the Internet. Perhaps that is the most important insight regarding Web 2.0 and how webmasters should prepare. I am already working on renewing and improving some projects, not because I want to label them with new versions, but because I firmly believe that the only constant should be change, and on the Internet, change must occur more frequently. *By Christian Van Der Henst S.* And if you’re still not sure, here’s this video. Great! http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NLlGopyXT\_g
